html:
For other members of the Berwald family, see Berwald.

Franz Adolf Berwald, born July 23, 1796, in Stockholm, and died April 3, 1868, in Stockholm, was a Swedish composer of German descent, cousin to the composer and violinist Johan Fredrik Berwald, and grandfather to the pianist Astrid Berwald. In addition to music, Berwald dedicated himself to orthopedic work and "mechanical physiotherapy," which was groundbreaking for its time, and influenced by Nils Åkerman. The new concert hall built in Stockholm in 1976 for Swedish Radio was named Berwaldhallen after Franz Berwald. Berwaldhallen was inaugurated on November 30, 1979.

== Biography ==

=== Childhood and Youth === Franz Berwald was born in Stockholm as the son of violinist Christian Friedrich Georg Berwald and Brita Agneta Bruno, the latter belonging to a Swedish Västergötland family and being a descendant of the Bure clan. The Berwald family originally came from Neumark, and several of its members have been prominent musicians or composers. Franz's father, Christian Friedrich, was a chamber musician for Duke Karl and founded the music library at the royal court. He gave his son a free upbringing and took him out of school due to corporal punishment. From the age of five, the son began playing the violin, taught by his father. Because his father worked at the court, Franz made his debut there as early as nine years old, and the following year he had already given several concerts in other places as well.

In 1810, Franz Berwald became a student of Edouard Du Puy, and in 1812, he received an appointment from him as first violinist at the royal court orchestra. Berwald attempted to support himself by publishing magazines such as "Musikalisk journal" in 1819 and "Journal de Musique" in 1820, where he also published several of his own songs and piano pieces. After a couple of years as a freelance artist, during which time he undertook concert tours with his brother Christian August, he returned to a salaried position as an alto violinist in the royal court orchestra from 1824 to 1828. As a member of the royal court orchestra, he became acquainted with the most famous operas of the time. The first act of his first attempt at opera, "Gustaf Wasa," was completed in 1828. He traveled to Russia, Germany, Austria, Norway, and Finland to gain more musical inspiration.

Franz Berwald himself later became very critical of his works from his youth, and destroyed many of them. However, during the latter part of the 1810s, some of his works attracted attention, and he was considered a promising talent. With support from various sources, including Crown Prince Oscar (later King Oscar I), he went to Berlin in 1829, where he came into contact with, among others, Felix Mendelssohn.

=== Foreign Stays and Physical Therapy ===

The lukewarm reception in the Swedish music scene meant that Berwald had to find other ways to support himself. In Germany, he had become familiar with modern physiotherapy and opened a clinic in Berlin. He was very innovative in this field, as he was the first person in the world to use mechanical devices, which he invented himself, and he became highly regarded as a physiotherapist. From 1835 to 1841, he headed a medical gymnastics institute. He was at his most productive as a composer during the 1840s.

Despite his success as an orthopedist, he wanted to be a musician, and for this reason, he moved to Vienna in 1841. There, he completed the opera "Estrella di Soria" (1841), as well as the symphonies "Symphonie Sérieuse" in G minor and "Symphonie Capricieuse" in D major, both in 1842. Six "tone paintings" for orchestra were also composed during the years 1841-42. The works received attention, but did not generate enough financial income for him to support himself in Vienna. Despite his creative joy and rich cultural exchange in Vienna, he was forced to return to his homeland, where he was met with indifference by the Swedish music scene. The opera "Estrella di Soria" was not performed at the Stockholm Opera until 1862, and two operettas completely failed, despite the world-renowned soprano Jenny Lind singing the main role in one of them.

Once again, he tried his luck abroad, with stays in Paris, Vienna, and Salzburg between 1846 and 1849. He did not receive the same early recognition from the Swedish musical elite as he did, for example, in Austria, where he was elected an honorary member of the Mozarteum in Salzburg in 1847. His music was received with greater understanding abroad than in Sweden, but unfortunately, it did not provide enough financial return for him to support himself as a freelance musician. Once again, he returned to his homeland.

=== The later years ===

He unsuccessfully sought positions within the Swedish music scene, both as court conductor to replace his cousin and as director of music in Uppsala; he did not obtain either position. In 1850, he instead became the manager of the Sandö glass factory in the parish of Bjärtrå, a role he held until 1860, when he switched to working in a brickyard. He was finally elected as a member (no. 386) of the Royal Swedish Academy of Music on January 22, 1864, where he was tasked with revising Haeffner's hymn book. From 1867, he lectured on composition and instrumentation at the Music Conservatory. He also worked as a private tutor for periods, and among his students was Kristina Nilsson. His last theatrical work was "Drott."

How certain are we about this information?

Information about people from the 19th century is very well documented (through census records, newspapers, and letters). The uncertainty mainly concerns selection and perspective – whose stories are highlighted.
tldr: Franz Berwald was a Swedish musician and composer from the 19th century who also became known for inventing new methods of physiotherapy. A concert hall in Stockholm is named after him.
lattlast:
Franz Berwald was born in Stockholm in 1796. He grew up in a family of musicians and began playing the violin at a very young age. He worked for many years as a musician in the royal court orchestra, which was a group of musicians who performed for the king and other important figures.

Because he couldn't earn enough money by composing music alone, he started working as a physical therapist. This was a relatively new field at the time, and he invented his own machines to help people with injuries or movement problems. He achieved great success with this work in Germany.

Berwald traveled extensively to countries such as Germany, Austria, and Russia to study music and gain inspiration. Despite being a talented composer, his music did not receive as much attention in Sweden as it did abroad. In his later years, he worked as a teacher and private instructor.

Today, he is remembered through Berwaldhallen, a concert hall in Stockholm where music is performed for audiences.
